dataset.name: EID Quick Reference - User Account Control

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: UAC auto-elevation — process elevated without user prompt
entries.summary: A process was automatically elevated to administrator privileges by UAC without displaying a consent dialog, using the auto-elevation path reserved for signed Windows binaries.
entries.details:
Event 4 from the Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel records instances where UAC's auto-elevation mechanism granted a process administrator privileges without a user consent prompt. Auto-elevation is an intentional Windows design feature for certain digitally-signed Microsoft binaries (e.g., mmc.exe, eventvwr.exe, fodhelper.exe, sdclt.exe) that are trusted to run elevated without interrupting the user.

The message includes the executable path, the requesting user, and the resulting integrity level.

**Security relevance — UAC Bypass (T1548.002):**

Auto-elevation is the mechanism exploited by the majority of documented UAC bypass techniques. Attackers abuse auto-elevating binaries by:

1. **Registry-based COM hijacking:** Dropping a malicious COM server in HKCU (e.g., overriding the shell command for a progID or the InprocServer32 for a CLSID that an auto-elevating binary calls) so that the trusted binary auto-elevates and then executes the malicious COM object with administrator privileges. Classic examples: eventvwr.exe (HKCU\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command), fodhelper.exe (HKCU\Software\Classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command), sdclt.exe (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\App Paths\control.exe).

2. **DLL hijacking in elevated context:** Placing a malicious DLL in a directory searched before the legitimate DLL by an auto-elevating binary, so that the hijacked DLL executes with elevated privileges.

3. **Environment variable manipulation:** Some auto-elevating processes use environment variables to locate resources; manipulating these before the binary runs can redirect execution.

**Key detection signal:** EID 4 for any executable that is NOT in the expected set of Windows-signed auto-elevating binaries (or for binaries whose image path is not under System32/SysWOW64) indicates potential UAC bypass. Correlation with HKCU registry changes (Sysmon 13) in the 30 seconds before the auto-elevation is the strongest signal.

**Channel enablement:** The Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel is not enabled by default and must be manually enabled.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control

prerequisites.description: The Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events will be collected.

investigationPivots: Executable path — legitimate auto-elevation only occurs for digitally-signed Microsoft binaries under %SystemRoot%\System32 or %SystemRoot%\SysWOW64; any path outside these directories or any third-party executable is an immediate IOC
HKCU registry changes preceding the event — check Sysmon EID 13 for writes to HKCU\Software\Classes in the 60 seconds before the auto-elevation for COM hijacking evidence

Parent process — an auto-elevation spawned from an unusual parent (e.g., cmd.exe, wscript.exe, a non-administrative user context) is suspicious; Security 4688 / Sysmon 1 records the parent-child chain
User context — auto-elevation from a non-interactive or service account context is anomalous and warrants immediate review
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate Windows administrative tools (mmc.exe, eventvwr.exe, diskmgmt.msc) auto-elevating during normal administrative work
Software installers and updaters from Microsoft that are signed and meet the auto-elevation criteria
Windows Update and servicing processes that use auto-elevation during patch installation

keyFields.name: ExePath
keyFields.description: Full path of the auto-elevated executable; non-System32/SysWOW64 paths are the primary IOC

keyFields.name: UserName
keyFields.description: The user account under which the auto-elevation occurred; service accounts or unexpected users are high-priority

detectionRules.title: UAC Auto-Elevation Outside System32 — Possible UAC Bypass
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Provider_Name == "Microsoft-Windows-UAC"
| where EventID == 4
| parse EventData with * "<Data Name='ExePath'>" ExePath "</Data>" *
| where ExePath !startswith @"C:\Windows\System32"
      and ExePath !startswith @"C:\Windows\SysWOW64"
      and ExePath !startswith @"C:\Windows\WinSxS"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ExePath, EventData
detectionRules.notes: This rule will produce minimal noise since legitimate auto-elevation is almost exclusively from System32. Any hit outside those paths warrants investigation. Tune path exclusions for your environment if custom Microsoft-signed tooling is deployed.

detectionRules.title: UAC Auto-Elevation Preceded by HKCU Registry Write — COM Hijack Pattern
detectionRules.rule:
let AutoElevation = Event
| where Provider_Name == "Microsoft-Windows-UAC"
| where EventID == 4
| project ElevationTime = TimeGenerated, Computer;
let HKCUWrite = Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon"
| where EventID == 13
| parse EventData with * "<Data Name='TargetObject'>" RegKey "</Data>" *
| where RegKey has "HKCU\\Software\\Classes"
| project RegWriteTime = TimeGenerated, Computer, RegKey;
AutoElevation
| join kind=inner HKCUWrite on Computer
| where RegWriteTime < ElevationTime and RegWriteTime > datetime_add('second', -60, ElevationTime)
| project ElevationTime, Computer, RegKey, RegWriteTime
detectionRules.notes: This correlation rule is high-fidelity for registry-based UAC bypass techniques. Requires Sysmon with registry monitoring enabled (Event ID 13). The 60-second window covers the typical exploit pattern where the registry key is written and the bypass binary is immediately invoked.

detectionRules.title: UAC Auto-Elevation Event
detectionRules.rule:
title: UAC Auto-Elevation Event
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: uac-operational
    definition: Requires Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel enabled via wevtutil
detection:
    selection:
        Provider_Name: Microsoft-Windows-UAC
        EventID: 4
    filter_system32:
        ExePath|startswith:
            - 'C:\Windows\System32\'
            - 'C:\Windows\SysWOW64\'
            - 'C:\Windows\WinSxS\'
    condition: selection and not filter_system32
falsepositives:
    - Microsoft signed tools outside System32 (rare)
level: high
tags:
    - attack.privilege_escalation
    - attack.defense_evasion
    - attack.t1548.002

entries.title: UAC consent prompt displayed to user
entries.summary: UAC displayed an elevation consent dialog to the user for a process requesting administrative privileges, recording whether the user approved or denied.
entries.details:
Event 5 from the Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel is logged each time the UAC consent UI (consent.exe) presents an elevation prompt to the user. The event records the executable path requesting elevation, the requesting user, the privilege requested, and the user's response (approved or denied).

This event is the interactive counterpart to EID 4 (auto-elevation). Unlike auto-elevation, a consent prompt requires the user to acknowledge the elevation — meaning this event documents deliberate user choices to grant or refuse administrator access.

**Security relevance:**

1. **Elevation of unexpected binaries:** An attacker who has achieved code execution as a standard user may attempt to social-engineer the user into approving a UAC prompt for a malicious binary. EID 5 with an approved response for an unusual executable (not a recognized installer or management tool) is a potential indicator of this technique.

2. **Frequency anomalies:** Legitimate users only see UAC prompts during software installation, system configuration changes, or administrative tasks. Unusual frequency — particularly from a non-technical user's workstation — may indicate malicious software repeatedly requesting elevation or a user approving prompts without reading them.

3. **Denied elevations from non-standard binaries:** A denied EID 5 still records the binary path. Repeated denial for the same unusual binary indicates a persistent elevation attempt the user is (so far) refusing.

4. **Consent prompt from an unexpected location:** The consent.exe prompt should only be initiated by the Application Information service (AIS). Any process directly invoking the elevation pathway for a binary outside of program installation directories warrants scrutiny.

**Channel enablement:** The Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel is not enabled by default.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control

prerequisites.description: The Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events will be collected.

investigationPivots: Executable path in the consent prompt — installers in %TEMP%, %APPDATA%, or user-writable directories are suspicious; expected elevation sources are %ProgramFiles% or %SystemRoot%
User response — an approved prompt for a suspicious binary is higher priority than a denied one, but both indicate the elevation attempt occurred
Frequency — more than 2-3 EID 5 events per day for a non-administrative user is anomalous on a managed workstation
Correlation with Security 4688 / Sysmon 1 — confirm what process started immediately after an approved consent (EID 5 approved → elevated process creation should follow within seconds)
commonFalsePositives: Software installation and updates initiated by users (legitimate installers triggering UAC prompts)
Administrators performing routine system administration tasks that require elevation
IT deployment tools (e.g., PDQ Deploy, SCCM user-initiated installs) that prompt for UAC confirmation

keyFields.name: ExePath
keyFields.description: Path of the executable requesting elevation; the primary triage field — binaries in user-writable directories are high-priority

keyFields.name: UserName
keyFields.description: The user who was prompted; non-admin users receiving elevation prompts for non-installer binaries warrant investigation

keyFields.name: Decision
keyFields.description: Whether the user approved or denied the elevation prompt; approved elevations for suspicious binaries are immediately actionable

detectionRules.title: UAC Consent Prompt for Binary in User-Writable Location
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Provider_Name == "Microsoft-Windows-UAC"
| where EventID == 5
| parse EventData with * "<Data Name='ExePath'>" ExePath "</Data>" *
| where ExePath has_any (@"\AppData\", @"\Temp\", @"\Users\", @"\ProgramData\")
      and ExePath !has @"\AppData\Local\Microsoft\"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ExePath, EventData
detectionRules.notes: Elevation prompts for binaries in user-writable paths (AppData, Temp, ProgramData) are high-priority. Tune the exclusion list for legitimate tools deployed by your IT team to user profile directories.

detectionRules.title: UAC Consent Prompt Presented to User
detectionRules.rule:
title: UAC Consent Prompt Presented to User
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: uac-operational
    definition: Requires Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel enabled via wevtutil
detection:
    selection:
        Provider_Name: Microsoft-Windows-UAC
        EventID: 5
    filter_programfiles:
        ExePath|startswith:
            - 'C:\Program Files\'
            - 'C:\Program Files (x86)\'
            - 'C:\Windows\'
    condition: selection and not filter_programfiles
falsepositives:
    - User-initiated software installations from Downloads or Desktop
    - IT-managed tools deployed to non-standard paths
level: medium
tags:
    - attack.privilege_escalation
    - attack.t1548.002

entries.title: Application Information service started
entries.summary: The Application Information (AIS) service, which underpins UAC elevation prompts and token filtering, has started successfully.
entries.details:
Event 33 records the successful startup of the Application Information service (AppInfo / AIS). The AIS service is the core Windows service that manages UAC elevation requests: it is responsible for running consent.exe for interactive prompts, handling auto-elevation for approved binaries, and creating elevated tokens for approved processes.

**Security relevance:**

The Application Information service is a prerequisite for UAC to function. Some UAC bypass and privilege escalation techniques target the AIS directly:

1. **Tampering baseline:** EID 33 documents when the AIS starts. Under normal conditions the service starts at boot and runs continuously. Unexpected startups (AIS stopping and restarting mid-session, visible as an EID 34 followed by EID 33) may indicate the service was manipulated.

2. **Post-tampering recovery:** If an attacker attempts to disable or modify the AIS to weaken UAC enforcement, and a security tool or administrator restarts the service, EID 33 records the recovery timestamp.

3. **AIS as a bypass target:** Several documented techniques (e.g., DLL injection into the AIS process, COM object hijacking that the AIS invokes) rely on compromising the AIS process. A start event after an unexpected stop is a pivot point for investigating AIS process integrity.

**Normal pattern:** EID 33 appears once at system boot. Any subsequent EID 33 (after initial startup) is worth correlating with EID 34 (service stop) and System 7036 (service state change) to understand why the service was restarted.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control

prerequisites.description: The Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events will be collected.

investigationPivots: Is this the first EID 33 after boot, or a subsequent restart? Check for a preceding EID 34 (AIS service stop) to determine whether the service cycled unexpectedly
Correlation with System 7036 — confirms the service state change and records whether it was a normal start or a recovery from a failure
Process creation context — check Sysmon 1 for svchost.exe hosting the AppInfo service; unexpected parent or command-line arguments for the hosting svchost are anomalous
commonFalsePositives: Normal system startup — EID 33 once per boot is expected
Service recovery after an unexpected crash of the AIS (Windows automatic service recovery restarts it)

keyFields.name: Message
keyFields.description: Confirms AIS startup; security value is in timing and recurrence rather than event content

detectionRules.title: Application Information Service Restart Mid-Session
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Provider_Name == "Microsoft-Windows-UAC"
| where EventID == 33
| join kind=inner (
    Event
    | where Provider_Name == "Microsoft-Windows-UAC"
    | where EventID == 34
    | project StopTime = TimeGenerated, Computer
) on Computer
| where TimeGenerated > StopTime and TimeGenerated < datetime_add('minute', 5, StopTime)
| project RestartTime = TimeGenerated, Computer, StopTime
detectionRules.notes: AIS restarting within 5 minutes of a stop event (outside of system boot) is anomalous. The first EID 33 after boot will not have a corresponding EID 34 in the same session, so this query naturally filters to mid-session restarts.

detectionRules.title: Application Information Service Started
detectionRules.rule:
title: Application Information Service Started
status: informational
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: uac-operational
    definition: Requires Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel enabled via wevtutil
detection:
    selection:
        Provider_Name: Microsoft-Windows-UAC
        EventID: 33
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Normal system boot
level: informational
tags:
    - attack.defense_evasion
    - attack.t1548.002

entries.title: Application Information service stopped
entries.summary: The Application Information (AIS) service, which underpins UAC elevation functionality, has stopped — an unexpected stop outside of system shutdown may indicate UAC enforcement disruption.
entries.details:
Event 34 records the stop of the Application Information service (AppInfo / AIS). Under normal circumstances, the AIS runs continuously from boot to shutdown and is not stopped except during system restart or explicit service management by an administrator.

An unexpected AIS stop (EID 34 without a corresponding system shutdown event in the System log) is a significant security indicator:

1. **UAC enforcement disruption:** When the AIS is stopped, UAC elevation prompts cease to function. Processes that would normally trigger a consent prompt or auto-elevation will instead fail or run without elevation, depending on how the calling process handles the error. Some malware stops the AIS to prevent elevation prompts from appearing while it operates, effectively silencing UAC without the more obvious step of changing UAC policy.

2. **Precursor to privilege escalation:** Stopping the AIS and then restarting it with a modified DLL or COM registration loaded (via DLL hijacking in the AIS process) is a documented privilege escalation technique. The pattern is: EID 34 (stop) → malicious DLL/COM placement → EID 33 (start with compromised state).

3. **Administrative bypass:** An administrator with sufficient privileges can stop the AIS using `sc stop AppInfo` or `net stop AppInfo`. This is a known step in some administration scripts but is rarely necessary in normal operations.

**Normal pattern:** EID 34 should only appear at system shutdown (immediately before a System 6006/1074 shutdown event). Any mid-session EID 34 is anomalous.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control
Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

prerequisites.description: The Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel is disabled by default and must be enabled before events will be collected.

investigationPivots: Mid-session EID 34 — immediately check whether a system shutdown event (System 6006/1074) follows within 60 seconds; if not, the AIS was stopped while the system was running
Process that stopped the service — correlate Security 4688 / Sysmon 1 for sc.exe, net.exe, or PowerShell with 'stop AppInfo' or 'stop appinfo' arguments around the same timestamp
Follow-up EID 33 — a stop followed by a restart suggests deliberate manipulation; check for new or modified DLLs loaded by the restarted AIS process via Sysmon 7 (image loaded)
commonFalsePositives: Normal system shutdown — EID 34 immediately followed by a System 6006/1074 shutdown event is expected behaviour
Service recovery restart after an unexpected AIS crash (Windows SCM automatically restarts the service)

keyFields.name: Message
keyFields.description: Confirms AIS shutdown; security value is in timing — mid-session occurrence is an IOC

detectionRules.title: Application Information Service Stopped Mid-Session
detectionRules.rule:
let AISStop = Event
| where Provider_Name == "Microsoft-Windows-UAC"
| where EventID == 34
| project StopTime = TimeGenerated, Computer;
let SystemShutdown = Event
| where EventID in (1074, 6006)
| project ShutdownTime = TimeGenerated, Computer;
AISStop
| join kind=leftouter (SystemShutdown) on Computer
| where isnull(ShutdownTime) or abs(datetime_diff('second', StopTime, ShutdownTime)) > 60
| project StopTime, Computer
detectionRules.notes: This query identifies AIS stops that are NOT followed by a system shutdown event within 60 seconds — the hallmark of a mid-session service stop. Requires the System log to be collected alongside UAC Operational events.

detectionRules.title: AIS Service Stop via Command Line
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4688
| where CommandLine has_any ("AppInfo", "appinfo")
| where CommandLine has_any ("stop", "Stop")
| where ParentProcessName !has "services.exe"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, CommandLine, ParentProcessName
detectionRules.notes: Detects explicit service stop commands targeting AppInfo. Legitimate service management from services.exe (Windows SCM) is filtered. Commands from cmd.exe, PowerShell, or other parents warrant investigation.

detectionRules.title: Application Information Service Stopped
detectionRules.rule:
title: Application Information Service Stopped
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: uac-operational
    definition: Requires Microsoft-Windows-UAC/Operational channel enabled via wevtutil
detection:
    selection:
        Provider_Name: Microsoft-Windows-UAC
        EventID: 34
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Normal system shutdown sequence
level: high
tags:
    - attack.defense_evasion
    - attack.privilege_escalation
    - attack.t1548.002
    - attack.t1562.001
